    Home › Ships › Madrid Mærsk

    Madrid Mærsk

    Maersk · Container · In service · built by Hanwha Ocean (DSME)

    Madrid Mærsk opened the second Triple-E generation in 2017 and carried Maersk past the 20,000 TEU line for the first time.

    Length overall399 m
    Beam58.6 m
    Gross tonnage214,286 GT
    Speed22 kn
    Delivered2017
    Draft16.5 m
    Our numbers

    Derived metrics

    Computed by ships.fyi from the core specification — see how we calculate these.

    TEU per metre#8 of 13
    51.5 TEU/m nominal TEU ÷ length overall
    Length-to-beam ratio#23 of 45
    6.81 length overall ÷ beam
    Sense of scale

    How big is it, really?

    The Madrid Mærsk's length overall, against things you already know the size of.

    Madrid Mærsk399 m
    Eiffel Tower (height)330 m
    Titanic269.06 m
    Football pitch105 m
    Boeing 747-876.3 m
    Blue whale25 m
    You (probably)1.75 m

    The 747-8 bar is the longest airliner ever built — our sibling site aircraft.fyi measures everything that flies the same way.

    One Madrid Mærsk = 1.48 Titanics · 3.8 football pitches · 16 blue whales · 5.2 Boeing 747-8s laid end to end.

    #17 of 45 by length → #17 by gross tonnage → Run the Canal-Fit Checker →
    Specification

    The numbers

    Length overall≈399 m
    Beam58.6 m
    Capacity20,568 TEU
    Gross tonnage214,286 GT
    DeliveredApril 2017
    FlagDenmark

    Compare it

    Put the Madrid Mærsk next to something

    vs Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller →vs OOCL Hong Kong →
    vs Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller vs MOL Triumph vs Emma Mærsk Compare with anything →
    ← PreviousLegend of the Seas Next →Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller

    Last verified: 2026-07-15 · Spot an error? business@luck.fyi

    The story

    What makes it different

    Madrid Mærsk is the quiet record-holder of the 2017 size war: delivered days after MOL Triumph became the first ship rated past 20,000 TEU, she carried a higher figure — 20,568 — and briefly made Maersk the owner of the world's largest ship again.

    She leads the Triple-E Mark II generation: the same efficiency-first philosophy as Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller with the deckhouse pushed higher and forward to stack one more tier of containers, squeezing over two thousand extra TEU from essentially the same hull envelope.

    The capital-city-named class became eleven strong, and today they run inside the Gemini Cooperation — the hub-and-spoke network Maersk launched with Hapag-Lloyd in 2025 that reorganised the Asia–Europe trade around schedule reliability.

    The class

    The Madrid Mærsk line

    Triple-E Mk II
    Lead of eleven second-generation Triple-Es (2017–19), all named for capital cities, with a raised bridge and extra tier of boxes over the first generation.
    Operators

    Who operates the Madrid Mærsk

    All 1 operator of record, verified 2026-07-15. Figures marked ≈ are approximate.

    MSKMaerskAsia–Europe service; now operating within the Gemini network with Hapag-Lloyd.1✓→
    Quick answers

    Madrid Mærsk, asked and answered

    How long is the Madrid Mærsk?
    399 m (1,309 ft) length overall — the #17 longest of the 45 ships on ships.fyi, and 3.8 football pitches end to end.
    How big is the Madrid Mærsk compared to the Titanic?
    The Madrid Mærsk is 1.48× the Titanic's length — 399 m against her 269.06 m. By volume the gap is starker still: 214,286 GT against Titanic's 46,328 GRT — roughly 4.6 times the tonnage, measured across two different rule eras.
    Who operates the Madrid Mærsk?
    Maersk. Current status: in service.
    Does the Madrid Mærsk fit through the Panama Canal?
    No. At 399 m long, 58.6 m wide and 16.5 m of draft, the Madrid Mærsk exceeds even the Neopanamax gauge (366 × 51.25 × 15.2 m) — run any gauge on the Canal-Fit Checker.

Port safety Ships in storm The Silhouette Quiz Methodology Matchups Icon vs Titanic Legend vs Icon Titanic vs Queen Mary 2 Seawise Giant vs Icon Ever Given vs Ever Alot Emma Mærsk vs MSC Irina Blog The six days that stopped world trade Why the biggest ships stopped growing
    About the data. Every specification is compiled from operator, builder and registry sources and cited per row. Fleet and status figures are snapshots, marked ≈ where approximate and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. Ranks never mix units — cruise ships rank by GT, container ships by TEU, tankers by DWT. All silhouettes are original works of ships.fyi, drawn to true scale. Guides may contain clearly marked affiliate links; they never change what we recommend. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
    © 2026 ships.fyi
